Extremely hot take (as in not thought-through and certainly limited): I'm philosophically opposed to artists presenting their work as off-limit from AI generative big models.

Makes total sense, mind you, that an artist want to protect their work from plagiarism or false attribution. My bone to pick is with the initial posturing we're starting to get from artists asking to be put on a "blacklist" of automated prompts.

Creating an artwork is a balance between your ownership of it and the ripples it has across the social milieu wherein you produce it. AI art draws on the latter, i.e., on the part where it already stopped being solely yours.

Abusive comparison, but heartfelt: wanting your style to be inaccessible by ML is tantamount to the copyrighting of Vantablack by Anish Kapoor. Wanting to slow the inevitable disowning and mutation of a cultural artifact that you *de facto* entrusted to the rest of mankind.
@MasterScrat Again, reminder that my stance is a) thoroughly uncooked, b) not a jab against the artist in question, who is totally in her right: https://t.co/viN5SyNoS5

@kortizart First, to answer your question, I count 366 dead artists / 407 living artists in the Stable Diffusion database, and 1435 living artists/ 1439 dead artists in the wider Disco Diffusion database. Obviously, there are more artists, but that's the part we can actually gauge

@kortizart Second, I empathize with you. Truly, I do. Feeling like anybody could coopt your style with a click, especially without effort or other intents than financial gain, is a depersonalizing experiment. Not trying to deny that in the least.

@kortizart However, these models, like photography were in their time, are the hallmarks of something that already happens in our brain. We store and manipulate all images we've seen in our heads. The AI does the same.

@kortizart If and when I inputed "Karla Ortiz" in the AI, it wouldn't solely rely on what it scraped from your public accessible works on the internet. It would draw from all the works labelled "Karla" and "Ortiz" in its 6 billion database.

@kortizart The current entanglement of AI with financial corporations, imo, belies something about it that is way more altruistic: it gives us access to a latent space wherein all works that connected several humans can be mapped together, and mobilized as material.

@kortizart That's precious. That's unownable. And while it shouldn't be used to transgress a late or living artist's will about their legacy, it is my belief that limiting its reach to dead artists (who can't opt out on their own) is giving the copyright system far more power than opposite

@kortizart At any rate, that's also why it's important to research the domain at this stage. As long as the process remains a black box only used by convinced people, the larger public won't have an enlightened discussion about the implications for artists and their wages. Hence my charts

@kortizart Though from there, it could evolve either way. You have in mind the curbing of copyright by companies, I have in mind artists like Anish Kapoor or Yves Klein using the law to exclude anyone else from using a certain shade of black or blue.

@kortizart And who knows? Maybe in time, we'll reach an optimal solution: public domain allowed for generation, models helping artists to use a dataset of their own works to generate stuff, and maybe a search engine allowing living artists to be more widely known
@kortizart Ofc it was totally clear ! If you want to continue this conversation later, here or by DM, I'd really like this. Good night ☺️

And I feel somewhat legitimate in feeling slighted. I'm just an undergrad with barely the average number of followers on twitter. Facing a person with an audience of 100x the size is relatively nerve-wracking. Being told I'm not qualified, or sincere, is discouraging.

I wish to state that our exchanges started after her use of a statistic post I made, which led to some of my friends being harassed in pretty nasty ways. This habit of QTs where a manikin of "thieving slimy tech bros" is brandished, with fact-checking/nuance hidden in replies

Is not really nice, and not really productive. Stuff might get lost in internet translation, but I also perceive some kind of tacit will of not giving an inch to people you either deemed as corrupt or misguided. Which I'm desperate to prove we aren't.

I think AI art is warranted to receive critical inquiry as art, as I believe that artists like Maurizio Cattelan are fully artists, even by having someone tape a banana to a wall. Art can be unethical, especially when unregulated, which Ai can't and won't remain. But it's art.

That is my opinion, at least. But as someone who likes Gell, and wishes to see art in an anthropological way, I'm merely waiting for use and time to decide. Like for photography, disc-jokeying, ready-made, you name it. It's society who will choose.

But I will not let myself be intimidated out of the conversation. Because I am qualified for this. Because the number of persons who both know about conceptual art and AI art beyond "it's prompt-ism" is slim. Doesn't give me authority. But I think what I bring is valid.

Just as photographers face the issue of being both the height of reality depiction, yet needing hard work to express themselves through this media, AI artists grapple w/ a core dichotomy: a medium most responsive to one's intent AND running counter to hasty notions of authorship https://t.co/wzHDmPBuDB

@leeahdg I think there's a gradient running from "artists who use a medium to convey a message" to "artists who take the medium as the object of their message." Newness makes AI users skew towards the latter 😊

@leeahdg Just like you can comment on photography with other media (pop art comes to mind), yeah. I use "medium" for the means of producing a work, and the characteristics of it that are instrumental in shaping that work

@MystWolven Mostly about the idea that being denied the status of artists by some actors in the scene doesn't seem to hinder or demoralize users of AI, which it might have. No dismissal of other concerns, unless you can point it to me
@eldritch48 Thanks, really means a lot to me. It's taxing and I certainly miss a lot of my swings, but as long as it can bring a bit of clarity to some people, I'll strive to do better 😊

@leeahdg Both ! Painting and photographing the same person with the same photorealistic intent produces two different results : you wield different competences, each slight alteration of the process makes it unique. AI, with its settings, prompts and post prod, has its own

@leeahdg And most of all, committing to paint/drawing in a world of pictures is also a meaningful choice. What do you want to capture that a lens can't ? The answer makes your art unique and gives it a message. AI asks "Why do you need to call on this database ?" And goes from there

@MystWolven As you maybe have seen in my thread, I stricly separate the ontological and ethical aspects of ML content synthesis. Ethically, I am quite certain the current state of AI models will face judicial consequences. But unregulated art remains art, and ethically sourced AI art is art

@leeahdg Just like most pictures we consume don't answer to an artistic need: we take them to capture a moment, or to fill a social account. But AI adds the interplay of speech and sight, which I simply couldn't achieve without a model that contains so many words and images. An example -

@leeahdg - is this. I used a negative prompt, so the model "substracted" the elements of the lower box from the elements of the first. Both taken from a study by Karandashev and al. (2019), about the factors people tend to ascribe to love https://t.co/2dzh8VWFz0

@MystWolven That's perhaps my favorite line of discussion, actually ! started doing AI by adapting lines from @gods_txt, a bot trained from several occult or religious texts. Never one-to-one, but the core of the prompt was itself born of a machine https://t.co/suLnAeYQRS

@MystWolven @gods_txt Does that make me a "creator" ? "The" creator of the work ? Hard to say. Mostly, it shows that the notions we have for others products (commissioner of the work, consumer, artist) each have features I've had to have to produce this, but fail to capture the whole of it

@leeahdg The question is wider than AI art. Examples of from conceptual art (say, a banana taped to a wall) are illegible if they're only judged on visual merits. Backtrack to the 19th century, and you have Louis Leroy bashing on impressionism

@leeahdg So I would say that, indeed, someone with no knowledge of how I produced the image will either rate it too highly (as if I had painted it myself) or too low (as if the subject was trite, with no link to the prompt). In both case, they'll have seen the image, not the artwork.

@leeahdg Mostly goes to show that a new tool always gets a wide array of use cases, most of which derived from older mediums. After pictures left the hands of scientists, you had people using them to take portraits, the first doctored pictures being used for jokes or pranks, etc.

@leeahdg While the artist in the contest was open about his use of Midjourney, he still submitted it in a competition whose criteria certainly were not up to date with Midjourney. Only time, familiarity with the medium and history will tell which criteria will direct AI taste

@leeahdg But in all cases, uses almost always end up coexisting. You have graphic artists today at every level of the social  ladder, some amateurs, some commissioned, some only drawing nude females while others strive for poetic or weird or realistic feels - and that's okay !

@leeahdg Let me put it this way: while I think that, objectively and in all cases, medium and medium proficiency shapes the mind and the production of every artist (Photoshop, AI or oil paint), I think that there's going to be some artists who define themselves primarily by AI & some not

@leeahdg Again, really great question ! I would hope that they'll take a closer look at the process. And not only for doctored content passed as real. AI shows that each image is a unique possession of a latent field of images. We'll never see them all: hence, those we will are precious

@leeahdg Also, the awareness of the long strenuous process of graphic art will certainly gain awareness. Now that we actually can gauge what we get from one button press vs the more adaptable, in-depth result of a graphic designer, this'll also reflect in the audience's expectations

@leeahdg And again, the fundamental question of "why?", which gives meaning to it all. "I made this unicorn, because the unicorns that the database produce don't reflect the one I imagined. Or because I wanted to use only retro tools, digital or not."

@leeahdg The flood of new media, from printing to picture (and even the written word), sadly tends to have this impact. But that very problematic can be dealt with if social actors bring it to light. As you said, AI art can very well be critiqued by a trad artist through their work

@leeahdg And I think I'll can an art tech that is freely available, even to people who criticize it, than a world where only the socially powerful, rich or educated can use it or have a say in it. (Already the case with computer, mind you: try being a graphic designer in Burundi)

@MBulteau @advadnoun Negative prompts may ask the boundaries of concept definition. Interpolation add the concepts of time, fluidity, and iteration. Inpainting and outpainting, extending or rewriting parts of an image, has the same interests as a collage - a dialogue between captation and poiesis.

@MBulteau @advadnoun If none of those notions ring a bell to you, that's okay ! But it can explain why you fail to grasp the cultural relevance of the medium. It doesn't mean it's deprived of any - but that you might need to realize what praxis are involved in its making to assess said relevance
RT @images_ai: This is an essay by @StevenZapata about his issues with AI art. It's been out for less than two weeks, has over 140,000 view…

@MBulteau @advadnoun No harm done: like I said, not having those informations with a medium in its infancy is clearly not inexcusable. But delving into it is IMO required to have a fuller opinion, i.e. care about art. (Also as I know him, quite sure @advadnoun didn't imply the latter assumption)

@MBulteau @advadnoun Will not dispute that, of course, although I'd argue the current landscape of art, driven by both tech and trends to its level of industrialization, already might be parsed as abusive. The fact that AI-opponents usually don't seems a mix between a) the charge being led by (1/2)

@MBulteau @advadnoun artists being advantaged in the current ecosystem, and "smaller" artists anxious for such a spot to vanish before they've ever had the chance to reach it, and 2) a rhetorical professed love for the manual craft as more soulful, or less exploitative in and of itself

@MBulteau @advadnoun To sum things, we have two potential outcomes: the Shutterstock/Spotify routes, addressing emotional concerns, but paying artists in pennies and cementing IP in the hands of the powerful. Or the route of photography and mixing: in the hands of all, w/ its own value system

@MBulteau @advadnoun Obviously, the "haha-suck-it illustrator" is not productive at best, malicious at worst. But I'd say it is so more in its formulation than in its diffuse, ill-formulated intent: as a response to illustrative artists, relying on tools once seen as soulless and industry-breaking.

@MBulteau @advadnoun The fact we see Photoshop as a tool, and not a creator to be credited or an unfair "creation dope" may lie less in salient observations than a sense of diffuse continuity: as long as we're talking brushstrokes and self-expression, art is safe, and we can sleep soundly...

@MBulteau @advadnoun Whereas devices like the camera (or, in your field, the synthesizer, maybe an even apter metaphor), both influence the craft of their user significantly, and alter for ever the production standards in their fields

@MBulteau @advadnoun As I said elsewhere, it might seem disingenuous of AI art adepts to bring up ontological arguments ("AI art is art because overruling it discards much prior artistic production) in the econ. question ("Will AI art put artists out of business). However, at least to me...

@MBulteau @advadnoun Opinions in the latter are rooted in the former. If AI art proves an exploit to the current model of art monetization, which many recognize as flaw, it's that model I'd wish to see called into question

@MBulteau @advadnoun Of course, this is where your liberty as a creator has to be enforced, through society's legal consensus. If it were up to me, your melodies would be protected from misattribution, but you releasing it as public would open them to those wishing to emulate them, by hand or machine

@MBulteau @advadnoun Luckily, we don't leave in such a world, and I do believe that the possible abuses I handwave in my philosophical position will be addressed in time. Although I still believe that we'd have much to gain by updating our system to AI, rather than the sole reverse

@EZE3D @GanWeaving Cut-up, both visual and textual, can involve randomness or blindness as a constraint - thats the core conceit of, say, the cadavre exquis. Like I said, various amount of plastic agency are a feature of different mediums - a painter has more than a photographer. Does it detract ?

@EZE3D @GanWeaving This is not my opinion, and I hardly see where I've implied this. As an art historian, our point of contention is that your arguments against AI being art exclude some of the very forms of traditional arts dear to the both of us.

@EZE3D @GanWeaving The discussion of AI being unethical and appropriative (and whether it's a transitory or permanent stage of the tech) is separate - though proving that AI can be a transformative tool detracts from its characterization as a simple appropriative force calling for stronger IP laws.

@EZE3D @GanWeaving Yeah, settling what is art is not feasible (and, obviously, can be irritating if used as a decoy not to address the danger to artists livelihood). However, the pointing-a-leaf-on-the-ground (or banana-taped-to-the-wall) ad absurdum is often used to disparage valid forms of art...

@EZE3D @GanWeaving ... while they should, instead, enlighten us to the true way out of this dilemma: art *is* about humans, and its weirdest or most "hands-off" forms only qualify inasmuch as they manage to relate to human questions and idiosyncrasies. Again, I include AI in this.

@EZE3D @GanWeaving That's exactly where my personal fears lie, though. If we imagine a world with, say, opt-in models, it would be easy for a fringe of big-conglomerates to monetize them (à la Spotify). Still impacts artists (downsizing, lower price + higher speed to compete with machines)...

@EZE3D @GanWeaving ... but also guarantees a tougher time for creators of fanworks (which I'm quite sure Disney or Marvel will have no qualms using AI to automatically flag), while fostering a toxic outlook towards anyone wanting to reclaim AI for their own.

@EZE3D @GanWeaving Agreed on art as human - still thking on the latter. Ready made- to mind: "final", non-creative physical pieces, staged in an artistic display, relying more on social performance. Which only a human can pull off convincingly, as part and formed by human society.

@bildoperationen The phenomenon is further affected by hidden default parameters: a base --stylization of 100 (out of 1000), and a "chaos" (the temperature of the model, for those familiar with ML terms) is set to 0. Non-Midjourney models, like Automatic1111, are  more upfront with these...

@bildoperationen Also, as general advice: do you use the same --seed (number) when you do these ? That adds academic reproducibility: otherwise, the variation from the parameter can be attributed to the random seed, and we'd need several outputs for each prompt to cancel out.

@CoffeeVectors I wanted to underline that there's many ways to read "AI" as terrain. @Orwelian84 proposed it as a tool / alternate to fieldwork: a cultural reflection thru outputs or training data. I lean more on the myths and discourses surrounding it (1/2)

@ChombaBupe We already had this formulated in history of art with Adorno's idea of the aura of unique objects... and it's unclear if it works. The Mona Lisa is unique, but its cultural impact is due to its countless repetitions as "poor images", in print, on the web, or even described

@ChombaBupe Also, the idea we are "wowed" by rarity can't be equated to commercial appeal in an era of mass-consumption : there's an anthropological appeal in belonging to a mass trend, from fandom to blockbusters. And what about meme culture ? Scarcity ≠ value there either

@ChombaBupe That kind of shifts the onus from rarity of the artifact to rarity of the *perception of its onset*... in a way that also has issues. Re: the mock Balenciaga photoshoot trends, born of the rare experience of having a say (and coopting the look) of two high-positional value IPs

@ChombaBupe That's where the idea of "rarity" breaks down. Cultural goods with high values in our times, especially IP (books, movies or graphic work) can be both highly concentrated and mass-produced and reproduced. Marvel is both a monopoly, and a multiverse multibranded hegemonic mess

@ChombaBupe And it decidedly breaks down if we use the idea of rarity to, say, claim GenAI allows to delve a corner of a given mediatic latent space that wouldn't have been reified otherwise, whereas non-generative art, as a life-earning wage, is constrained to do "more of the same"

@ChombaBupe I won't risk sealioning on the information part (I'm not qualified in it), but I  feel iffy with the idea of explaining taste to a single factor like rarity (evn subjective). Like when pseudo-scientists say women or men are "genetically" driven to certain markers bc reproduction

@ChombaBupe Like... saying "men love wide hips bc perceived fertility" has issues, even before empiric measurement. It fails to consider how historically relative body standards have been, it works with a mostly heteronormative framework, etc.

@ChombaBupe Claiming we can mostly chalk the formal variations and societal valuation of artworks to the sole criterion of perceived rarity... not to say it's *exactly* like that, but does it truly works for all ethnological contexts ?

@ChombaBupe It's possible I just don't see it! I read your claim as "AI is bound to produce things that humans will like less, because humans are attracted to events they perceive as scarce, cause they hold more information". Which is not necessarily the consensus in anthro or HA (my fields)

@ChombaBupe That's why I don't think it's really actionable in art! The Mona Lisa has a sea of reproductions: its composition is not a rare sensory to me when I perceive live at the Louvres. And in its context of production, the Quatrocentto, novelty was less valued than mimesis

@ChombaBupe I get that must be headbangingly frustrating to try to xplain it, but if the notion is a) abstract to the point of describing all sensory/aesthetic experiences, b) is iffy to adapt to past or present discourses in art, monetary/otherwise, maybe it's not the most actionable tool

@IrisVanRooij But in mine, it dredges up a whole host of biased centered discourses. For example, the fact that other cultures (ancient Greeks mostly) had different takes on randomness, authorship, creativity, beyond the cognitive frame. Or, for that matter, different models of personhood

@IrisVanRooij Caricaturally, I have the pro-AI crowd on my left saying "artificial means it's a perfect substitute of X", and the anti-AI going "you fools, we all know what X is, it's a REAL thing and this is SLOP". Meanwhile, X is just a social construct, and we're losing track of that

@IrisVanRooij (To be clear: cognition is an empiric measurement, but the notions of "creativity", "intelligence" or "authorship", which we in part build on top of the manifestations of cognitive functions, are constructs of discourse, i.e. in the anthro-ethno ballpark. If that makes sense)
